content.problemStatement:
Construct and return a binary tree using two integer arrays, `preorder` and `inorder`. The `preorder` array represents the preorder traversal of the binary tree, while the `inorder` array represents the inorder traversal of the same tree.

**Example:** 

`Preorder` array: [1, 2, 3, 4, 5] 

`Inorder` array: [2, 1, 4, 3, 5]

The constructed tree




Input Format

The first line of the input contains a single integer NNN — the number of nodes in the binary tree.
The next line contains NNN space separated integers, the preorder array.
The next line contains NNN space separated integers, the inorder array.


Output Format
Complete the given function in the IDE which returns the root of the constructed binary tree.
Output will be `Well Done!` if the tree is constructed correctly, otherwise Runtime errors.
content.inputFormat: First line contains an integer T, the number of test cases. Each test case consists of three lines: the first line contains an integer N — the number of nodes in the tree. The second line contains N space‑separated integers denoting the preorder traversal. The third line contains N space‑separated integers denoting the inorder traversal.
content.outputFormat: For each test case print a single line containing the string "Well Done!". The judge internally validates the constructed tree; the program only needs to output this line.

sampleTestCases.output:
Well Done!
Well Done!
Well Done!
sampleTestCases.explanation: Three test cases are processed. Each pair of traversals corresponds to a valid binary tree, so the program prints "Well Done!" for every case.
A single test case with N=4. The traversals describe a valid tree, therefore the output is "Well Done!".
Both test cases are valid (different shapes). The program confirms each by printing the success message.

hidden.label: stress/general multiple cases
hidden.expectedOutput:
Well Done!
Well Done!
Well Done!

solution.approach: The first element of the preorder array is always the root of the (sub)tree. Using a hash map we store the index of every value in the inorder array. The position of the root in inorder splits the array into left and right subtrees. The size of the left subtree tells us how many elements belong to the left part in preorder. Recursively repeat the process for left and right parts. This runs in O(N) time per test case because each node is visited once and all look‑ups are O(1). The recursion depth is at most N, so the auxiliary space is O(N) for the recursion stack and the hash map.
solution.timeComplexity: O(N) per test case, O(total N) over all test cases
solution.spaceComplexity: O(N) per test case for the hash map and recursion stack

code.Python3:
import sys
sys.setrecursionlimit(1_000_000)

def build(pre,pl,pr,inorder,il,ir,idx):
    if pl>pr:
        return None
    root=pre[pl]
    i=idx[root]
    left=i-il
    node={'val':root,'left':None,'right':None}
    node['left']=build(pre,pl+1,pl+left,inorder,il,i-1,idx)
    node['right']=build(pre,pl+left+1,pr,inorder,i+1,ir,idx)
    return node

def main():
    data=sys.stdin.read().strip().split()
    if not data:
        return
    it=iter(data)
    T=int(next(it))
    out=[]
    for _ in range(T):
        N=int(next(it))
        pre=[int(next(it)) for _ in range(N)]
        inorder=[int(next(it)) for _ in range(N)]
        idx={v:i for i,v in enumerate(inorder)}
        root=build(pre,0,N-1,inorder,0,N-1,idx)
        out.append('Well Done!')
    sys.stdout.write('\n'.join(out))
if __name__=='__main__':
    main()
